title: Setting up a rainwater harvesting system for stock
subtitle: Collecting roof run-off to supplement mains water for livestock.
excerpt: A rainwater collection system from a livestock building roof can supply a significant proportion of daily stock water requirements, reducing mains water use and providing a buffer during summer dry periods. This covers the basic components, the sizing calculation, and the limitations on using rainwater for drinking water.
sourceNotes: DEFRA farm water efficiency guidance. Environment Agency, rainwater harvesting for non-potable uses. ADAS, farm water management guide.

content.text: A standard livestock building with a 100 sq m corrugated metal roof can yield around 60,000 litres of rainwater per year in a typical UK rainfall area. The system has three parts: a downpipe diversion, a storage tank, and a gravity or pumped distribution line to the drinkers.

The minimum system needs a downpipe leaf filter to keep coarse debris out. It also needs a first flush diverter on each downpipe entering the tank. A covered storage tank (a 1,000 to 5,000 litre IBC or purpose-made tank) holds the water. An overflow pipe directs surplus to a soakaway or drainage channel. An outlet tap or automatic ball valve supplies the drinkers.

To estimate the monthly yield: roof catchment area in square metres multiplied by the average monthly rainfall in metres, multiplied by a run-off coefficient of 0.8 for metal roofing. For a 100 sq m roof and 50 mm of rainfall: 100 x 0.05 x 0.8 = 4,000 litres. Size the tank to store at least one month's expected yield for the driest month, which in the UK is typically July or August with 40 to 60 mm of rainfall.

Rainwater collected from agricultural roofs is suitable for watering stock, cleaning equipment, and irrigation, but is not treated drinking water. Algae growth in uncovered tanks reduces water quality; cover all tanks from light. Check the water regularly for unusual colour or smell. Connect the tank to the drinker system with a
at a higher elevation, or a low-pressure pump to maintain flow to drinkers.
